prior.content_md:
## The Regulation

The Advanced Clean Trucks regulation is a manufacturers' ZEV sales requirement adopted by CARB in June 2020 and in force beginning with the 2024 model year. It requires manufacturers that certify Class 2b–8 chassis or complete vehicles with combustion engines for sale in California to sell zero-emission trucks as an increasing percentage of their annual California sales, scaling through 2035.

Published sales percentage targets by 2035:
- **Class 2b–3 trucks:** 55% ZEV sales
- **Class 4–8 straight trucks:** 75% ZEV sales
- **Truck tractors (Class 7–8):** 40% ZEV sales

*Source: CARB Advanced Clean Trucks program page, California Air Resources Board. https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/our-work/programs/advanced-clean-trucks*

The regulation also contains a one-time large entity and fleet reporting requirement, under which large entities and fleets operating in California must report vehicle and operational data to CARB. This reporting obligation is distinct from the manufacturer sales mandate.

*Source: CARB Advanced Clean Trucks program page, California Air Resources Board. https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/our-work/programs/advanced-clean-trucks*

## Multi-State Reach Through Section 177 Adoption

Ten states beyond California have adopted ACT under Clean Air Act Section 177, extending the regulation's effective market coverage to approximately 40% of US truck sales:

- **2025 model year effective:** Massachusetts, New Jersey, New York, Oregon, Washington
- **2026 model year effective:** Vermont
- **2027 model year effective:** Colorado, Maryland, New Mexico, Rhode Island

*Source: CARB, "States that have Adopted California's Vehicle Regulations." https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/our-work/programs/advanced-clean-trucks (linked from program page)*

**Note:** The EPA's authority to grant California waivers under the Clean Air Act, which is the legal basis for multi-state ACT adoption, has been subject to ongoing federal policy tension. Legal Confirmation Required as to whether any federal executive actions taken in 2025–2026 have altered the waiver status or the enforceability of adopted state rules in the listed states. No authoritative published ruling confirming revocation or suspension of the California waiver for ACT has been cited in the available source pool as of April 2026; this gap should be tracked.

## Why This Applies to the Workspace

The workspace operates as a freight forwarder with road transport as a secondary mode. Road operations supporting the workspace's cargo verticals — live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, and humanitarian freight — rely on third-party drayage and over-the-road carriers. These carriers are subject to ACT's manufacturer-driven ZEV ramp in California and adopting states, and in California they are separately subject to the Advanced Clean Fleets regulation.

The mechanism of exposure is indirect but material:

- **Cause:** ACT mandates progressively higher ZEV proportions of new truck sales from manufacturers; ACF (companion regulation) requires fleet operators in California to transition away from diesel trucks on defined timelines.
- **Mechanical consequence:** The available pool of diesel-capable Class 7–8 drayage and over-the-road trucks for hire at California ports and inland terminals will progressively narrow; carriers unable to invest in ZEV equipment face compliance risk under ACF; carriers that do invest face higher capital costs.
- **Effect on the workspace's verticals:** Drayage cost and availability for live events, luxury goods, high-value automotive, and film/TV cargo moving through California ports (Los Angeles, Long Beach) or requiring road legs within California will be affected. Effect magnitude and timing require carrier-specific data that is not publicly available as a standardized rate; this is labeled a research gap.

*Source: CARB Advanced Clean Trucks program page. https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/our-work/programs/advanced-clean-trucks*

---
